revision.text:
{{神社 |名称 = 大歳金刀比羅神社 |画像 = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
鳥居と参道
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} |所在地 = [[兵庫県]][[多可郡]][[多可町]]中区鍛冶屋856-1 | 緯度度 = 35 | 緯度分 = 3 | 緯度秒 = 56.1 | N(北緯)及びS(南緯) = N | 経度度 = 134 |経度分 = 55 | 経度秒 = 45.7 | E(東経)及びW(西経) = E | 地図国コード = JP | 地図 = Japan Hyogo |祭神 = [[大歳神]]、[[大己貴命]]、[[保食神]]、[[事代主命]] |神体 = |社格 = [[村社]] |創建 = [[1795年]]（[[寛政]]6年） |本殿 = [[入母屋造]][[平入]] |別名 = 鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん |札所等 = |例祭 = 11月23日 |神事 = }} '''大歳金刀比羅神社'''（おおとしこんぴらじんじゃ）は、[[兵庫県]][[多可郡]][[多可町]]中区にある[[神社]]。 == 概要 == 古くは'''大歳神社'''として[[鍛冶]]の祭神である[[天目一箇神|天目一命]]が祀られていた。創立時期は不詳ながら、[[延宝検地]]（[[延宝]]5年）の検地帳に[[慶長]]年間に行われた検地で九畝二十歩の[[神田]]を有していたことが記されているため、慶長以前の創始と考えられている。[[1795年]]（寛政6年）に鍛冶屋村の藤井孫右衛門が諸病平癒のため、[[讃岐国]][[金刀比羅宮]]から金刀比羅神社を境内に勧請、大歳神社の[[摂社]]とした。 [[明治維新]]後の[[1874年]]（[[明治]]7年）には、[[近代社格制度]]に基づき[[村社]]となった。[[1911年]]（明治44年）、腐朽が進んだため大歳神社の社殿を除却、跡地に[[1881年]]（明治18年）に再建されていた金刀比羅神社の本殿を移設して合祀、社名を'''大歳金刀比羅神社'''に改めた。 == 社殿 == * 本殿 : 本殿の構造は[[入母屋造]][[平入]]で[[檜皮葺]]、規模は正面三軒、身舎側面二軒である。正面に[[千鳥破風]]および[[唐破風|軒唐破風]]をつけた一軒の庇が設けられており、三宝廻縁、浜床を有する。軒まわり、縁まわりには多数の装飾彫刻が施されており、当地における19世紀から20世紀初頭にかけての社寺建築の装飾意匠の変遷において参考となる遺構であるとされている。 * 旧拝殿 : 大歳神社の拝殿を移築したものである。構造は入母屋造、長床式、[[瓦葺き|桟瓦葺]]で、規模は正面三軒、側面二軒である。後世の補修により各所の原型は失われているが、細部の手法から19世紀初頭の建立と考えられ、前代の社殿の概略の参考となるとされている。 ファイル:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|本殿 == 境内社 == * 大神宮社 * [[天目一箇神|天目一神社]] * [[八坂稲荷神社]] * 北播護国神社 == 祭礼 == * 例祭（11月23日） : [[姫路市|姫路]]の[[播磨国]]総社[[射楯兵主神社]]、[[北条町 (兵庫県)|北条]]の[[住吉神社 (加西市)|住吉神社]]と並んで播州三大祭と称され播州三大祭の定義は諸説あり、このほか[[佐保神社]]、[[大宮八幡宮 (三木市)|大宮八幡宮]]、[[高砂神社]]、[[宗佐厄神八幡神社]]、[[松原八幡神社]]などが挙げられる。、かつては祭の期間中に10万人規模の参拝者があった。[[鍛冶屋線]]（1990年廃止）では参拝客輸送のため、車両を増結した5-6両編成での運行が行われていた。 == 脚注 == {{脚注ヘルプ}} {{reflist|refs= 中町史（p687） 中町史（p688） }} == 参考文献 == * {{Cite |和書 | author = 中町史編纂委員会 | title = 中町史 | publisher = 中町 | volume = 本編 | date = 1991-08 | id = {{全国書誌番号|025041376}} }} == 外部リンク == {{commonscat|Otoshi-Konpira jinja}} * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6312065.html 兵庫県神社庁 - 大歳金刀比羅神社] {{神道 横}} {{デフォルトソート:おおとしこんひらしんしや}} [[Category:兵庫県の神社]] [[Category:兵庫県の旧村社]] {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:多可町の建築物]] [[Category:多可町の歴史]]

revision.text:
{{Shrine | Name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | Image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | Location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | Coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | AlternateName = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira Shrine (Kagawa)|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * **Main Hall** : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * **Former Worship Hall** : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * **Annual Festival (November 23)** : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Ishikuta Heiza Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= Chūmachi-shi (p. 687) Chūmachi-shi (p. 688) }} == Bibliography == * {{Cite |Japanese book | author = Chūmachi-shi Compilation Committee | title = Chūmachi-shi | publisher = Chūmachi Town | volume = Main Volume | date = 1991-08 | id = {{NDL|025041376}} }} == External Links == {{commonscat|Otoshi-Konpira jinja}} * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6312065.html Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association - Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Former Sonsha]] [[Category:Taka Town Buildings]] [[Category:Taka Town History]] [[ja:大歳金刀比羅神社]]

{{Shrine | Name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | Image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach

It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * **Annual Festival (November 23)** : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Ishikuta Heiza Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. {{Shrine | Name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | Image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | Location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | Coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | AlternateName = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira Shrine (Kagawa)|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * **Main Hall** : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * **Former Worship Hall** : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * **Annual Festival (November 23)** : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Ishikuta Heiza Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= Chūmachi-shi (p. 687) Chūmachi-shi (p. 688) }} == Bibliography == * {{Cite |Japanese book | author = Chūmachi-shi Compilation Committee | title = Chūmachi-shi | publisher = Chūmachi Town | volume = Main Volume | date = 1991-08 | id = {{NDL|025041376}} }} == External Links == {{commonscat|Otoshi-Konpira jinja}} * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6312065.html Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association - Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Former Sonsha]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Taka Town Buildings]] [[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:new pages]][[Category:Taka Town History]] [[ja:大歳金刀比羅神社]]

Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Shrines in Hyōgo Prefecture]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Former Sonsha]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Taka Town Buildings]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]][[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:Taka Town History]] [[Category:pages to try to fix ills]] [[ja:大歳金刀比羅神社]]

revision.text:
{{Shrine | Name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | Image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | Location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | Coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | AlternateName = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira Shrine (Kagawa)|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''Annual Festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Ishikuta Heiza Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= Chūmachi-shi (p. 687) Chūmachi-shi (p. 688) }} == Bibliography == * {{Cite |Japanese book | author = Chūmachi-shi Compilation Committee | title = Chūmachi-shi | publisher = Chūmachi Town | volume = Main Volume | date = 1991-08 | id = {{NDL|025041376}} }} == External Links == {{commonscat|Otoshi-Konpira jinja}} * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6312065.html Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association - Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages with {{Shrine | Name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | Image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | Location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | Coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | AlternateName = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira Shrine (Kagawa)|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''Annual Festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Ishikuta Heiza Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= Chūmachi-shi (p. 687) Chūmachi-shi (p. 688) }} == Bibliography == * {{Cite |Japanese book | author = Chūmachi-shi Compilation Committee | title = Chūmachi-shi | publisher = Chūmachi Town | volume = Main Volume | date = 1991-08 | id = {{NDL|025041376}} }} == External Links == {{commonscat|Otoshi-Konpira jinja}} * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6312065.html Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association - Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages with Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''Annual Festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Ishikuta Heiza Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''Annual Festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Ishikuta Heiza Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= Chūmachi-shi (p. 687) Chūmachi-shi (p. 688) }} == Bibliography == * {{Cite |Japanese book | author = Chūmachi-shi Compilation Committee | title = Chūmachi-shi | publisher = Chūmachi Town | volume = {{Shrine | Name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | Coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | AlternateName = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira Shrine (Kagawa)|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''Annual Festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Ishikuta Heiza Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= Chūmachi-shi (p. 687) Chūmachi-shi (p. 688) }} == Bibliography == * {{Cite |Japanese book | author = Chūmachi-shi Compilation Committee | title = Chūmachi-shi | publisher = Chūmachi Town | volume = revision.text:
{{Shrine | Name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | Coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | AlternateName = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira Shrine (Kagawa)|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''Annual Festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Ishikuta Heiza Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= Chūmachi-shi (p. 687) Chūmachi-shi (p. 688) }} == Bibliography == * {{Cite |Japanese book | author = Chūmachi-shi Compilation Committee | title = Chūmachi-shi | publisher = Chūmachi Town | volume = Main Volume | date = 1991-08 | id = {{NDL|025041376}} }} == External Links == {{commonscat|Otoshi-Konpira jinja}} * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6312065.html Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association - Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Pages with redlinks to fill]][[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:Pages with redlinks to [[ja:大歳金刀比羅神社]]

{{Shrine | Name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | Coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | AlternateName = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''Annual Festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= Chūmachi-shi (p. 687) Chūmachi-shi (p. 688) }} == Bibliography == * {{Cite |Japanese book | author = Chūmachi-shi Compilation Committee | title = Chūmachi-shi | publisher = Chūmachi Town | volume = Main Volume | date = 1991-08 | id = {{NDL|025041376}} }} == External Links == {{commonscat|Otoshi-Konpira jinja}} * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6312065.html Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association - Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kk]] Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''Annual Festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= Chūmachi-shi (p. 687) Chūmachi-shi (p. 688) }} == Bibliography == * {{Cite |Japanese book | author = Chūmachi-shi Compilation Committee | title = Chūmachi-shi | publisher = Chūmachi Town | volume = Main Volume | date = 1991-08 | id = {{NDL|025041376}} }} == External Links == {{commonscat|Otoshi-Konpira jinja}} * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6312065.html Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association {{Shrine | Name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | Coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | AlternateName = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''Annual Festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= Chūmachi-shi (p. 687) Chūmachi-shi (p. 688) }} == Bibliography == * {{Cite |Japanese book | author = Chūmachi-shi Compilation Committee | title = Chūmachi-shi | publisher = Chūmachi Town | volume = Main Volume | date = 1991-08 | id = {{NDL|025041376}} }} == External Links == {{commonscat|Otoshi-Konpira jinja}} * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6312065.html Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association - Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:original pages]] [[Category:kk]] {{Shrine | Name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | Coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | AlternateName = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''Annual Festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= Chūmachi-shi (p. 687) Chūmachi-shi (p. 688) }} == Bibliography == * {{Cite |Japanese book | author = Chūmachi-shi Compilation Committee | title = Chūmachi-shi | publisher = Chūmachi Town | volume = Main Volume | date = 1991-08 | id = {{NDL|025041376}} }} == External Links == {{commonscat|Otoshi-Konpira jinja}} * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6312065.html Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association - Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:unsorted link categories]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:unsorted link {{Shrine | Name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | Coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | AlternateName = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''Annual Festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= Chūmachi-shi (p. 687) Chūmachi-shi (p. 688) }} == Bibliography == * {{Cite |Japanese book | author = Chūmachi-shi Compilation Committee | title = Chūmachi-shi | publisher = Chūmachi Town | volume = Main Volume | date = 1991-08 | id = {{NDL|025041376}} }} == External Links == {{commonscat|Otoshi-Konpira jinja}} * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6312065.html Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association - Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:kk]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''Annual Festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= Chūmachi-shi (p. 687) Chūmachi-shi (p. 688) }} == Bibliography == * {{Cite |Japanese book | author = Chūmachi-shi Compilation Committee | title = Chūmachi-shi | publisher = Chūmachi Town | volume = Main Volume | date = 1991-08 | id = {{NDL|025041376}} }} == External Links == {{commonscat|Otoshi-Konpira jinja}} * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6312065.html Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association {{Shrine | Name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | Coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | AlternateName = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | Alternatename = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= Chūmachi-shi (p. 687) Chūmachi-shi (p. 688) }} == Bibliography == * {{Cite |Japanese book | author = Chūmachi-shi Compilation Committee | title = Chūmachi-shi | publisher = Chūmachi Town | volume = Main Volume | date = 1991-08 | id = {{NDL|025041376}} }} == External Links == {{commonscat|Otoshi-Konpira jinja}} * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6312065.html Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association - Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]][[category:unsorted completion revision.text:
{{Shrine | name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | Alternatename = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= Chūmachi-shi (p. 687) Chūmachi-shi (p. 688) }} == Bibliography == * {{Cite |Japanese book | author = Chūmachi-shi Compilation Committee | title = Chūmachi-shi | publisher = Chūmachi Town | volume = Main Volume | date = 1991-08 | id = {{NDL|025041376}} }} == External Links == {{commonscat|Otoshi-Konpira jinja}} * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6312065.html Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association - Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Otoshi Konpira Shrine]][[Category:pages needing {{Shrine | name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | Alternatename = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= Chūmachi-shi (p. 687) Chūmachi-shi (p. 688) }} == Bibliography == * {{Cite |Japanese book | author = Chūmachi-shi Compilation Committee | title = Chūmachi-shi | publisher = Chūmachi Town | volume = Main Volume | date = 1991-08 | id = {{NDL|025041376}} }} == External Links == {{commonscat|Otoshi-Konpira jinja}} * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6312065.html Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association - Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:pages needing attribution]][[Category:Otoshi Konpira Shrine]][[Category:pages needing
