revision.comment: Removing from [[Category:Pages needing attribution]] on using [[c:Help:Cat-a-lot|Cat-a-lot]]
revision.text:
{{Shrine | name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | Alternatename = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= Chūmachi-shi (p. 687) Chūmachi-shi (p. 688) }} == Bibliography == * {{Cite |Japanese book | author = Chūmachi-shi Compilation Committee | title = Chūmachi-shi | publisher = Chūmachi Town | volume = Main Volume | date = 1991-08 | id = {{NDL|025041376}} }} == External Links == {{commonscat|Otoshi-Konpira jinja}} * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6312065.html Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association - Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:ZZZZZZZZZ Unlinked]] [[Category:Otoshi Konpira Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Otoshi Konpira {{Shrine | name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | Alternatename = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代社格制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= Chūmachi-shi (p. 687) Chūmachi-shi (p. 688) }} == Bibliography == * {{Cite |Japanese book | author = Chūmachi-shi Compilation Committee | title = Chūmachi-shi | publisher = Chūmachi Town | volume = Main Volume | date = 1991-08 | id = {{NDL|025041376}} }} == External Links == {{commonscat|Otoshi-Konpira jinja}} * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6312065.html Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association - Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Otoshi Konpira Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:Otoshi Konpira Shrine]][[Category:Otoshi It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= Chūmachi-shi (p. 687) Chūmachi-shi (p. 688) }} == Bibliography == * {{Cite |Japanese book | author = Chūmachi-shi Compilation Committee | title = Chūmachi-shi | publisher = Chūmachi Town | volume = Main Volume | date = 1991-08 | id = {{NDL|025041376}} }} == External Links == {{commonscat|Otoshi-Konpira jinja}} * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6312065.html Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association - Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Otoshi Konpira Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-Otoshi Konpira Shrine-qq]] [[Category:Otoshi revision.text:
{{Shrine | name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | Alternatename = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代type制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= Chūmachi-shi (p. 687) Chūmachi-shi (p. 688) }} == Bibliography == * {{Cite |Japanese book | author = Chūmachi-shi Compilation Committee | title = Chūmachi-shi | publisher = Chūmachi Town | volume = Main Volume | date = 1991-08 | id = {{NDL|025041376}} }} == External Links == {{commonscat|Otoshi-Konpira jinja}} * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6312065.html Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association - Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine] {{Shinto Horizontal}} {{shinto-stub}} [[Category:Otoshi Konpira Shrine]][[Category:Unsorted completion categories]] [[Category:qq-{{subst:MAINSPACE}}--qq]] {{Shrine | name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | Alternatename = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代type制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{Shrine | name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | Alternatename = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代type制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= Chūmachi-shi (p. 687) Chūmachi-shi (p. 688) }} == Bibliography == * {{Cite |Japanese book | author = Chūmachi-shi Compilation Committee | title = Chūmachi-shi | publisher = Chūmachi Town | volume = Main Volume | date = 1991-08 | id = {{NDL|025041376}} }} == External Links == {{commonscat|Otoshi-Konpira jinja}} * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6312065.html Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association {{Shrine | name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | Alternatename = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代type制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= Chūmachi-shi (p. 687) Chūmachi-shi (p. 688) }} == Bibliography == * {{Cite |Japanese book | author = Chūmachi-shi Compilation Committee | title = Chūmachi-shi | publisher = Chūmachi Town | volume = Main Volume | date = 1991-08 | id = {{NDL|025041376}} }} == External Links == {{commonscat|Otoshi-Konpira jinja}} * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6312065.html Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association {{Shrine | name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | Alternatename = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代type制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{Shrine | name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | Alternatename = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代type制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= Chūmachi-shi (p. 687) Chūmachi-shi (p. 688) }} == Bibliography == * {{Cite |Japanese book | author = Chūmachi-shi Compilation Committee | title = Chūmachi-shi | publisher = Chūmachi Town | volume = Main Volume | date = 1991-08 | id = {{NDL|025041376}} }} == External Links == {{commonscat|Otoshi-Konpira jinja}} * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6312065.html Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association {{Shrine | name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | Alternatename = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代type制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= Chūmachi-shi (p. 687) Chūmachi-shi (p. 688) }} == Bibliography == * {{Cite |Japanese book | author = Chūmachi-shi Compilation Committee | title = Chūmachi-shi | publisher = Chūmachi Town | volume = Main Volume | date = 1991-08 | id = {{NDL|025041376}} }} == External Links == {{commonscat|Otoshi-Konpira jinja}} * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6312065.html Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association {{Shrine | name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | Alternatename = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代type制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= Chūmachi-shi (p. 687) Chūmachi-shi (p. 688) }} == Bibliography == * {{Cite |Japanese book | author = Chūmachi-shi Compilation Committee | title = Chūmachi-shi | publisher = Chūmachi Town | volume = Main Volume | date = 1991-08 | id = {{NDL|025041376}} }} == External Links == {{commonscat|Otoshi-Konpira jinja}} * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6312065.html Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | Alternatename = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代type制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | Alternatename = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代type制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | Alternatename = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代type制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= Chūmachi-shi (p. 687) Chūmachi-shi (p. 688) }} == Bibliography == * {{Cite |Japanese book | author = Chūmachi-shi Compilation Committee | title = Chūmachi-shi | publisher = Chūmachi Town | volume = Main Volume | date = 1991-08 | id = {{NDL|025041376}} }} == External Links == {{commonscat|Otoshi-Konpira jinja}} * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6312065.html Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代type制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | Alternatename = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代type制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= Chūmachi-shi (p. 687) Chūmachi-shi (p. 688) }} == Bibliography == * {{Cite |Japanese book | author = Chūmachi-shi Compilation Committee | title = Chūmachi-shi | publisher = Chūmachi Town | volume = Main Volume | date = 1991-08 | id = {{NDL|025041376}} }} == External Links == {{commonscat|Otoshi-Konpira jinja}} * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6312065.html Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | Alternatename = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代type制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | Alternatename = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代type制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
Torii and Approach
{{maplink2|frame=yes|plain=yes|type=point|zoom=12|marker=religious-shinto|frame-align=center|frame-width=270|coord={{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E}}}} | location = [[Hyōgo Prefecture|Hyōgo]], [[Taka District|Taka]], [[Taka Town|Taka]], Naka-ku Kajiyama 856-1 | coordinates = {{coord|35|3|56.1|N|134|55|45.7|E|region:JP|display=inline,title}} | Deities = {{ill|Ōtoshi no Kami|ja|大歳神}}, {{ill|Ōkuninushi|ja|大己貴命}}, {{ill|Ukemochi|ja|保食神}}, {{ill|Koto-shiro-nushi|ja|事代主命}} | Classification = {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} | Established = [[1795]] (Kansei 6) | MainHall = {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} | Alternatename = Kajiyama Kotohira-san (鍛冶屋のこんぴらさん) | AnnualFestival = November 23 | Rituals = }} '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine''' (大歳金刀比羅神社) is a {{ill|Shinto|ja|神道}} shrine located in {{ill|Taka Town|ja|多可町}}, Naka-ku, Hyōgo Prefecture. == Overview == Historically referred to as '''Ōtoshi Shrine''', it originally enshrined {{ill|Amenomahitotsu|ja|天目一箇神}}, the deity of blacksmithing. The establishment date is unknown; however, the shrine's land records from the {{ill|Enpō|ja|延宝}} period (Enpō 5) mention ownership of nine tan and 20 bu of {{ill|Shinden|ja|神田}} dating back to land surveys in the {{ill|Keichō|ja|慶長}} era, suggesting an earlier origin. In {{ill|1795|ja|寛政6年}} (Kansei 6), Fujii Magorōemon of Kajiyama Village invited the spirit of {{ill|Kotohira-gū|ja|金刀比羅宮}} from {{ill|Sanuki Province|ja|讃岐国}} to cure illnesses, establishing a {{ill|Sessha|ja|摂社}} within the grounds of Ōtoshi Shrine. After the {{ill|Meiji Restoration|ja|明治維新}}, the shrine was designated as a {{ill|Sonsha|ja|村社}} in 1874 (Meiji 7) under the {{ill|Modern shrine ranking system|ja|近代type制度}}. In 1911 (Meiji 44), due to decay, the main hall of Ōtoshi Shrine was dismantled, and the main hall of Kotohira Shrine, reconstructed in 1881 (Meiji 18), was relocated to the site. The shrine was renamed '''Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine'''. == Shrine Buildings == * '''Main Hall''' : The main hall follows an {{ill|Irimoya-zukuri|ja|入母屋造}} structure with {{ill|Hira-iri|ja|平入}} roofing and {{ill|Hiwadabuki|ja|檜皮葺}}. Its dimensions are three ken at the front and two ken at the side. It features {{ill|Chidori Hafu|ja|千鳥破風}} and a {{ill|Karaha Hafu|ja|唐破風}}, with eaves extending one ken. The structure includes triple balustrades and a raised floor. Richly adorned with carvings, it serves as an important example of local shrine architecture from the 19th to early 20th centuries. * '''Former Worship Hall''' : This is the original worship hall of Ōtoshi Shrine, relocated and repurposed. It has an irimoya-zukuri structure, a long-floor plan, and {{ill|Sangawara Buki|ja|桟瓦葺}} roofing. The size is three ken at the front and two ken at the side. Though altered by later repairs, it is believed to have been built in the early 19th century, providing insight into earlier shrine architecture. File:Otoshi-konpira - Honden.jpg|Main Hall == Sub-Shrines == * Daijingū Shrine * {{ill|Amenomahitotsu Shrine|ja|天目一神社}} * {{ill|Yasaka Inari Shrine|ja|八坂稲荷神社}} * Kitahari Gokoku Shrine == Festivals == * '''festival (November 23)''' : Known as one of the "Three Great Festivals of Harima Province," alongside the festivals of {{ill|Itatehyōzu Shrine|ja|射楯兵主神社}} in Himeji and {{ill|Sumiyoshi Shrine (Kasai City)|ja|住吉神社 (加西市)}} in Kitahari.Other interpretations of the "Three Great Festivals" include those of {{ill|Saho Shrine|ja|佐保神社}}, {{ill|Ōmiya Hachiman Shrine (Miki City)|ja|大宮八幡宮 (三木市)}}, {{ill|Takasago Shrine|ja|高砂神社}}, {{ill|Munetsa Yakujin Shrine|ja|宗佐厄神八幡神社}}, and {{ill|Matsubara Hachiman Shrine|ja|松原八幡神社}}. In the past, the festival attracted up to 100,000 visitors. During the operation of the {{ill|Kajiyama Line|ja|鍛冶屋線}} (discontinued in 1990), trains were extended to five or six cars to accommodate the influx of pilgrims. == References == {{Reflist|refs= Chūmachi-shi (p. 687) Chūmachi-shi (p. 688) }} == Bibliography == * {{Cite |Japanese book | author = Chūmachi-shi Compilation Committee | title = Chūmachi-shi | publisher = Chūmachi Town | volume = Main Volume | date = 1991-08 | id = {{NDL|025041376}} }} == External Links == {{commonscat|Otoshi-Konpira jinja}} * [http://www.hyogo-jinjacho.com/data/6312065.html Hyōgo Prefecture Shrine Association {{Infobox religious building| religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | name = Ōtoshi Kotohira Shrine (大歳金刀比羅神社) | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] |image = [[File:Otoshi-konpira - Torii.jpg|270px]]
